data.title: Scarcity, Strategy And Sovereignty: The Geopolitical Rise Of Commodities
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Samuel Rines, & Nitesh Shah

Picture this: it's 2025, and the world is still grappling with fragile supply chains, inflation that refuses to ease and geopolitical tensions that keep redrawing the map of global trade. In times like these, one asset class—often overshadowed in tech-driven bull markets—has stepped back into the spotlight: commodities.

Think of commodities not just as raw materials, but as the pulse of the global economy. The race for green energy has put an unprecedented squeeze on critical metals, making supply chains for lithium, copper and rare earths geopolitical chess pieces. Agricultural commodities have taken center stage as food security becomes a pressing concern for governments worldwide. And oil and gas? They're as unpredictable as ever, caught between economic pragmatism and climate ambitions.

For investors, this isn't just another cyclical trade. Commodities offer something different—seeking a hedge against inflation, a play on real assets and a way to gain exposure to some of the most powerful macro forces reshaping markets. With equity valuations hovering near historical highs and bond markets adjusting to shifting rate expectations, the case for commodities in portfolios has never been more compelling.

Trump 2.0

Trade

There will be tariffs and the threat of tariffs. Importantly, the Trump Administration has already declared an emergency at the Southern border allowing tariffs to be implemented through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. This does not require Congress and allows the Executive branch to place tariffs as it sees fit. While some actions have been announced and delayed (Mexico and Canada) or cancelled (Colombia), others have been implemented (China). The announcement of reciprocal tariffs is important due to its near blanket applicability. Instead of attempting to front-run or predict the policy shifts and targets, it is worthwhile to step back and understand that tariffs will continue to be a tool of negotiation for the White House. Tariffs and the threat of tariffs are going to continue. There are few knowns around the trajectory of trade policy, but one is that "policy volatility" will persist.

There are many ways these policies filter into the commodity complex. Placing or threatening tariffs across certain commodity products—including crude steel and aluminum - has caused prices to fluctuate meaningfully. This will cause various disruptions and opportunities for investors, requiring nimbleness and thoughtfulness as the headlines flow through markets.

In figure 1, growth in commodity prices and growth in global exports volumes from 1980 to 2024 are displayed over each other. Figure 1 indicates that export weakness is usually coupled with commodity price weakness.

Figure 1: Export Volume and Commodity Price Performance

We believe there is significant potential for tariffs and trade frictions to be less severe than what is currently priced into commodity markets. However, for now, the uncertainty remains a downside risk for the broader commodity complex.

Tariffs could contribute to higher inflation in the U.S. while simultaneously depressing global commodity prices due to demand destruction. This dynamic could complicate the Federal Reserve's efforts to manage inflation, potentially leading to U.S. policy interest rates remaining elevated for a longer period.

Geopolitics

Geopolitical risk remains top-of-mind for many investors. The past five years have seen a pandemic, the Russian invasion of Ukraine and a war between Israel and Hamas. But geopolitical risk is not static. It changes and shifts rather quickly. The pandemic saw oil prices briefly go negative in the U.S., only to have oil prices surge back over $100 following the invasion of Ukraine and remain steady during the Israel-Hamas conflict. That is all in the past, and the relevant question is how the geopolitical landscape evolves.

Recently, there has been a steady decline in incremental geopolitical risk. Hamas and Israel agreed to a ceasefire. While the ceasefire is fragile, it is a signal of willingness to come to an agreement on both sides to end the current conflict. President Trump then announced conversations with Russian President Putin and Ukrainian President Zelensky to attempt to bring a détente to that conflict. That is not straightforward, and any agreement will take time. But it is - again - a signal of willingness. Both of these developments reduce the level of geopolitical risk in the system, easing the geopolitical price premium in oil, natural gas and gold.

Trump has also voiced disappointment over the European Union's insufficient purchase of U.S. natural gas. If Trump succeeds in ending the Russia-Ukraine war, U.S. sanctions on Russia would likely ease. This raises the question of whether Europe would follow suit. An end to the war could weaken the U.S.'s ability to pressure the EU into buying more American oil and gas, as Russia's gas supplies would become more readily available. Iranian sanctions will escalate under the new Administration. This could drive oil prices higher, encouraging OPEC members to offset this impact by increasing their supply - either explicitly or through non-compliance with production quotas.

China - Will the Bazooka Ever Come Out?

China, as the world's largest consumer of commodities, has cast a shadow over the commodity complex with its relatively poor economic performance in recent years. However, as figure 2 shows, commodities have still managed to perform well despite these challenges.

In previous economic cycles, China was quick to implement grand stimulus measures to boost demand, often through large-scale, commodity-intensive infrastructure and real estate projects. This time, however, the government and central bank have taken a far more measured approach, opting for piecemeal, micro-targeted stimulus initiatives.

While the government has managed to prevent a complete collapse of the real estate sector, the situation remains far from a meaningful recovery.

In figure 2, growth in commodity prices and a measure of Chinese economic growth from 2006 60 2024 are displayed over each other. Figure 2 indicates that Chinese economic weakness is usually coupled with commodity price weakness.

Figure 2: Li Keqiang Index and Commodity Prices

The Chinese government acknowledges the country's slower population growth and more mature economic stage, which are likely to cap housing demand. As a result, it is wary of triggering new real estate investment bubbles. Indebtedness is another key concern, particularly as local government debts have become more visible following their transfer to central government accounts through various debt swap arrangements.

In recent years, China has accelerated its net-zero emissions ambitions, investing heavily in the infrastructure needed for a clean-tech future. This focus has supported metal prices in the absence of significant demand growth from the real estate sector. We expect this approach to continue, given China's dominance in solar, battery and electric vehicle technology production. Potential U.S. tariffs could push China to further promote domestic adoption of these technologies, moving the country closer to energy independence.

If tensions escalate into a tit-for-tat trade war, China may impose additional export restrictions on critical materials. For example, in 2023, China restricted exports of gallium, germanium and graphite to the U.S., followed by antimony and superhard materials in 2024. While these measures were primarily responses to semiconductor trade disputes, similar restrictions could be applied to energy transition materials.

China's policy efforts are complicated by a depreciating yuan. The People's Bank of China (PBOC) manages the currency by setting a central reference point with a positive or negative 2% fluctuation range. To maintain stability, the PBoC has had to intervene heavily, which limits its capacity to lower interest rates. In September 2024, the government announced a pivot to boost growth, leading to an initial surge in Chinese equity markets and commodities. However, further action has been limited, likely constrained by ongoing currency pressures.

Gold

Gold was the best-performing metal of 2024, gaining 27% and reaching a new all-time high of $2,789/oz in October 2024. We believe gold will surpass $3,000/oz this year, even if the U.S. dollar remains strong, and bond yields stay elevated. However, if the U.S. dollar weakens and bond yields decline slightly, as consensus estimates suggest, gold could rise even further, potentially reaching $3,070/oz (as shown in figure 3). Notably, gold has managed to defy the twin headwinds of a strong dollar and high bond yields over the past few months.

Figure 3 shows historical gold prices from 2012 to 2024 and then WisdomTree's forecast to end 2025 in $/troy ounce.

Figure 3: WisdomTree Gold Price Forecast

Gold is widely viewed as a hedge against financial market turbulence. In December 2024 and early January 2025, equity markets experienced some volatility following the initial strong rally after Trump's election in November 2024. However, by the end of January, equity markets had recovered most of their losses.

The Israel-Hamas ceasefire and a potential resolution to the Ukraine-Russia war could pose risks to the geopolitical premium in gold. However, we believe these geopolitical concerns could easily be replaced by others, such as a marginalized Iran behaving more unpredictably.

Central bank demand for gold remains robust. Notably, the PBoC resumed gold purchases in November and December 2024 after a pause since May 2024. This reinforces our confidence that China remains committed to its currency diversification strategy, with gold serving as a primary tool for seeking diversification.

Strong U.S. Dollar

Historically, a strong U.S. dollar has been linked to weaker commodity prices. While this relationship has not always held true in the post-COVID era, the recent resurgence of dollar strength could once again put pressure on commodities.

In figure 4, the returns of a commodity price index and a dollar price index from 2001 to 2024 are displayed over each other. Figure 4 indicates that dollar strength is usually coupled with commodity price weakness.

Figure 4: Commodities and U.S. Dollar

Conclusions - The Benefit of a Broad Approach

As policies become clearer, we may find that our fears were overstated, potentially paving the way for a relief rally across the broader commodity complex. Additionally, it can be challenging to attempt to choose individual commodities, as they can be quite volatile.

The WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC) represents a broad basket of commodities and intends to provide broad-based exposure, primarily through investments in futures contracts, to the following four commodity sectors: energy, agriculture, industrial metals and precious metals. The Fund may also invest up to 10% of its net assets in any combination of shares of one or more exchange-traded products that primarily hold bitcoin and in bitcoin futures contracts. The Fund will not invest in bitcoin directly. The Fund will not invest directly in physical commodities.

Commodity investors have familiarity with the following benchmarks:

  • Bloomberg Commodity Index: The index is made up of 24 exchange-traded futures on physical commodities, representing 22 commodities, which are weighted to account for economic significance and market liquidity.1
  • S&P GSCI Index: Liquidity is built into the index by including only the futures contracts on physical commodities that meet the thresholds of the methodology. Further, each commodity is weighted by world production, adjusted for futures trading volume.2

Translating this into a tangible takeaway for investors: The S&P GSCI Index is well-known for having a particularly heavy exposure to oil. Many of us remember how the price of oil plummeted during the heart of the Covid-19 pandemic in March and April 2020—and this price has since recovered to more normal levels. There are times when oil dominates the commodity story…and there are times when it sits more in the background.

GCC's underlying commodity exposures, historically, look a lot more similar to those of the Bloomberg Commodity Index than those of the S&P GSCI Index. GCC is also seeking to be a bit forward-looking by including an exposure to bitcoin, which neither the S&P GSCI Index nor the Bloomberg Commodity Index currently include. Bitcoin's price can also generate rather extreme volatility in both directions - and this can be an important factor in explaining GCC's performance relative to these benchmarks.

Figure 5: Standardized Performance

Figure 6: The Array of Returns across Broad Commodity Strategies

As people look at the current backdrop and consider a commodity exposure, we believe that GCC's track record indicates the capacity to be a bit forward looking, including new things like bitcoin at the forefront rather than waiting for the broader market.

Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy Schwartz has served as our Global Chief Investment Officer since November 2021 and leads WisdomTree's investment strategy team in the construction of WisdomTree's equity Indexes, quantitative active strategies and multi-asset Model Portfolios. Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. He served as Director of Research from October 2008 to October 2018 and as Global Head of Research from November 2018 to November 2021. Before joining WisdomTree, he was a head research assistant for Professor Jeremy Siegel and, in 2022, became his co-author on the sixth edition of the book Stocks for the Long Run. Jeremy is also co-author of the Financial Analysts Journal paper "What Happened to the Original Stocks in the S&P 500?" He received his B.S. in economics from The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and hosts the Wharton Business Radio program Behind the Markets on SiriusXM 132. Jeremy is a member of the CFA Society of Philadelphia.

Samuel Rines, Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy ”.

Nitesh Shah, Director of Research, WisdomTree Europe

Original Post

data.title: Alpha Metallurgical Resources Q4: A Minor Loss, But That Is Reflected In The Price
data.description:
Overview

Alpha Metallurgical Resources, Inc. (NYSE:AMR) is the largest metallurgical (“met”) coal mining company in the United States. The company has 20 mines in the Eastern U.S. and some more minor infrastructure assets as well. The majority of its coal is sold on the export market, and the coking coal type is mixed.

I have covered AMR frequently over the last couple of years, and my prior articles can be found here. This article will be focused on the Q4 2024 result that was released earlier today. I also owned AMR for part of last year, and I once again bought the stock this morning.

Coking coal prices have been weak for over a year now, which has caused most coal mining stocks to be weak during the last year as well. The weakness has intensified over the last 3 months, where we can see AMR and a few other U.S. coal mining companies getting severely punished. AMR is down 43% in three months and as much as 67% since the peak about a year ago.

While some retracement was always due to happen when coking coal prices weakened, the weakness is more than I would have expected. Especially when we consider that the loss in Q4 was very minor, and the company has still been generating positive free cash flow.

Q4 2024 Result

AMR reported an adjusted EBITDA of $53M in Q4 2024, up 8% compared to the prior quarter. The net loss was $2M in the most recent quarter, which was $6M less than the prior quarter. The earnings per share were $-0.16, in line with the consensus estimate.

The sales volume was 4.1M tons in the most recent quarter, on par with Q3 2024. The sales price declined slightly to $128/t in Q4 from $133/t in Q3. The cash cost offset the price decline, and the cash margin was $19/t in Q4, compared to $18/t in the prior quarter. This is by no means a strong result, but not terrible either given the challenging coking coal market.

We can see that AMR still managed to generate positive free cash flow in the most recent quarter, even if it was barely free cash flow positive. Given how weak coal prices have been so far in 2025, we are likely to see more of the same or possibly slightly worse in the first half of 2025 for AMR.

However, the company is very well-capitalized. AMR has $6M in debt, a cash position of $482M, and $519M in total liquidity. So, things need to get quite a bit worse and stay like that for a while, for there to be any balance sheet concerns. AMR has so far paused its buyback program until we see healthier coking coal prices again.

Valuation & Conclusion

With the Q4 2024 result, AMR revised down its 2025 metallurgical coal sales to around 15M tons, and the company also increased the cash cost very marginally. The remaining guidance figures are unchanged.

In the figure below, I have used the latest share price of $143, the financials as of Q4 2024, the latest guidance, and a few coal price scenarios. At current prices, 2025 is likely to be about free cash flow neutral. AMR has so far priced 37% of sales at $131.73/t, but that includes 95% of the thermal byproduct sales at lower prices as well.

For anyone who thinks current coal prices will persist for a long time. AMR is probably not that appealing. With that said, the stock price has now declined so much that the market cap is only $1.9B and the enterprise value is $1.5B.

So, if coking coal prices recover, AMR offers substantial leverage to the upside, where the free cash flow yield can be 30+% in a more normalized market. Keep in mind that AMR is likely to be distributing 100% of free cash flow to shareholders then as well, even if the buybacks are presently paused.

There is always the risk of further downside, though, both for coal prices and the AMR share price. For me, the risk is clearly to the upside here, which is why I have been buying the stock today.

My expectation is that we will start to see material supply curtailments well before AMR's $519M liquidity position is drained. AMR is not the lowest-cost producer, but there will be other coal miners with financial leverage and higher operating expenses suffering far more in the current environment.

The coal market is cyclical, like all commodities, and I don't think extrapolating current price trends is the right approach. I rate AMR a strong buy at the current level, where the enterprise value was even before today's stock price decline at the lowest level in 3 years.

data.title: BeiGene: Q4 Results Impress With Positive Earnings Expected In 2025
data.description:
Last December, I wrote an article on BeiGene, Ltd. (NASDAQ:ONC) suggesting the firm as a strong buy due to the approval of Tevimbra in Europe and the US. The accompanying growth along with growing sales of Brukinsa, the company's best-in-class BTK inhibitor, were anticipated to lead the company to positive earnings, perhaps as soon as this year.

On Thursday, BeiGene reported impressive Q4 earnings, with total global revenues rising to $1.1B in Q4 and $3.8B for the full year, an increase of 78% and 55% respectively. In 2024, BeiGene achieved full-year positive non-GAAP operating income and guided for positive GAAP operating income and cash flow generation from operations in 2025. Thus, strong sales growth in Tevimbra and Brukinsa have positioned the company for profitability sooner than anticipated.

In November, BeiGene announced its intent to change its name to BeOne Medicines, and the stock symbol has changed since my last article. Also, the company is re-domiciling from the Cayman Islands to Switzerland. Historically, the company has run operating losses its entire history, thus the transition to profitability should not be overlooked as a catalyst to attract new buyers to the stock. Since its founding in 2010, BeiGene has transitioned to a global oncology powerhouse, although the company remains relatively unknown among retail investors.

Weighing BeiGene's coming profitability against a higher valuation as the stock has rallied, I still see the company as a BUY and raise my 12-month price target slightly to $305/share.

BeiGene: Q4 2024 Results and Outlook

BeiGene's CEO John Oyler began his remarks on the Q4 earnings call by highlighting the company's remarkable growth. BeiGene achieved $3.8B in revenue for 2024, driven by the company's newfound leadership in the BTK inhibitor market. 13 new molecular entities ((NMEs)) entered the clinic in 2024.

BeiGene was built to address long-standing challenges of return on investment in the pharmaceutical industry. Clinical trials represent 75% of the total cost to develop and commercialize an oncology medicine, and these have been rising substantially. It is now estimated that the cost of an oncology patient has risen to $250-$300k per patient, from $100k only a few years prior.

To address this issue, BeiGene has leveraged a global CRO-free clinical team, allowing for faster and more cost-effective operations. BeiGene often begins early clinical studies in China, leveraging its global footprint to allow approval in the US and EU, while moving faster and more cost-effectively. With the approval of Brukinsa, Tevimbra and Pamiparib, BeiGene has proven execution of this strategy.

The earnings call continued to describe a case study on how BeiGene executed on the design and approval of a best-in-class BTK inhibitor. While ibrutinib and acalabrutinib reached market before Brukinsa, scientists at BeiGene noted that serum exposure for these drugs were well below the drug's IC50. These are covalent drugs, thus the IC50 reflects the concentration at which 50% of the offending protein (BTK) is neutralized by covalent linkage to the drug.

Over the period following dosage, a patient taking Brukinsa can dependably see serum exposure greater than the IC50 all the time, compared with 30% or 20% of the time for ibrutinib and acalabrutinib respectively.

These data led BeiGene to bet that improved patient outcomes could be achieved with the better pharmacokinetic (PK) profile of Brukinsa. In a Phase 3 trial called ALPINE, Brukinsa demonstrated superior progression free survival (PFS) of 59% at 42 months versus only 32% for ibrutinib.

The improved clinical outcomes observed for Brukinsa motivated oncologists prefer Brukinsa over ibrutinib and acalabrutinib. Since ALPINE was reported and Brukinsa was approved by the FDA, the market share of BeiGene's drug increased.

Thus, the compelling clinical data led to exponential growth in sales for Brukinsa. Less than two years from approval and despite being third to market, Brukinsa has become the best-in-class and most prescribed BTK inhibitor on the market.

The company's scientific execution, particularly for Brukinsa, but also with Tevimbra, have driven rapid revenue growth. Q4 2024 saw $1.12B in revenues, up +77% from the same period in 2023. Full-year revenues of $3.78B increased by +73% versus 2023. For the full year, the US accounted for 52% of sales, while Europe represented 38% and the rest of the world 10%.

For the full year, Brukinsa achieved sales of $2.64B, or 69.9% of product revenues. Tevimbra achieved sales of $621M, or 16.4%. Andrew Berens of Leerink Partners has estimated peak sales of Brukinsa to reach $5.9B over the coming years. Tevimbra, a PD-1 inhibitor, faces competition from similar drugs on the market, but was still estimated to reach $1.7B in annual sales by 2030. This is substantially lower than Keytruda and Opdivo, which are forecast to reach peak sales of $23.2B and $9.1B, respectively.

For the coming year, guidance was issued expecting revenues of $4.9B to $5.3B and operating expenses of $4.1B to $4.4B. With GAAP gross margin projected in the mid-80% range, this forecast would lead to positive full-year GAAP profitability and generation of positive cash flow from operations.

Turning to coming catalysts from the pipeline, BeiGene is running numerous late-stage clinical trials on Tevimbra and a smaller number on Brukinsa. The company's strategy was not explicitly stated, however, it is assumed that BeiGene is attempting to maximize the sales of Tevimbra in the large PD-1 inhibitor market. This market is huge, with $41.8B in sales during 2023, largely dominated by Keytruda and Opdivo. Over the coming year, there are numerous clinical catalysts with the goal of expanding the total addressable market and positioning Tevimbra to compete, similarly to what BeiGene accomplished with Brukinsa.

In total, BeiGene's execution has been remarkable. Over the coming year, the major catalyst will be the growth of Tevimbra depending on the outcome of clinical trials. In addition, BeiGene has competed based on price, setting Tevimbra's price at a 10% discount to Keytruda. The coming studies will be interesting to see if a compelling superiority of Tevimbra can be established.

ONC Stock: Analyst Growth Projections

As of my previous writing, analysts had seen $4.64B in 2024 revenues. This has adjusted higher to $4.90B, the lower end of BeiGene's guidance for the coming year. Approximately 20% annualized growth is then expected for 2026 and 2027 as Brukinsa and Tevimbra continue toward peak sales.

These estimates seem very reasonable, however, moving forward, it is difficult to envision a major upside surprise without either successful commercialization of a new drug or Tevimbra capturing a larger share of the PD-1 inhibitor market than presently believed. This is in a nutshell why I see the stock as a buy rather than a strong buy currently.

Presently, analysts expect non-GAAP EPS to come in around $1.73/share in 2025. As growth continues in 2026 and 2027, earnings should expand significantly. Analysts see $8.48/share in EPS for 2027, or approximately 32.8x the current stock price.

Applying the midpoint of 12-month sales guidance ($5.1B) to my previous price/sales multiple for the company over the past 12-months (6.44x) leads to an implied market capitalization of $32.8B, a 10% premium to the current stock price. As such, I raise my 12-month price target slightly to $305/share.

Conclusion

BeiGene has executed impeccably on its strategy, with two products now approaching blockbuster status. Since my last article, the stock has rallied approximately 30% even as the S&P 500 has traded lower. While I expect this level of outperformance will be difficult to replicate without a further catalyst, I remain confident that with 13 NMEs in the clinic last year and further study on Tevimbra ongoing that a catalyst will emerge. BeiGene is a BUY for investors seeking aggressive growth in the healthcare space.

data.title: We Need To Talk About Coinbase
data.description:
Safety first

We have had the biggest cryptocurrency heist in history. $1.5 billion worth of Ethereum USD (ETH-USD) related tokens was lost from Bybit. The price of ETH-USD reacted with a 4% drop; however, it recovered in short order and is trading back at "its" levels. Bybit is the 4th largest exchange by volume traded in 24 hours. In response to this heist, the company received more than 350,000 withdrawal requests. However, theft on Bybit is not the only problem currently being addressed by the cryptocurrency world. Almost daily, we can read articles about the rug-pulls of weak personalities. Should we fear a similar situation with Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ:COIN)?

In my view, Coinbase is a relatively stable company when it comes to security. Of course, a similar situation can occur with Coinbase, but this company currently has no major scandal on its account. Coinbase has only had two major incidents so far, and all of them were related to phishing when hackers attacked a client. With the increasing price of crypto assets, it is necessary to count on more sophisticated attacks, even on companies like Coinbase. Therefore, it is necessary to constantly remind that the exchange does not hold large amounts of crypto assets.

Why might the time be right to buy COIN?

If we look a bit at the technical data, Coinbase stopped at exactly the 200-day moving average on Friday, February 21, 2025 after an 8.27% depreciation. At the moment, we are still in a bullish trend according to technical analysis. Most analysts still have a pessimistic outlook against Coinbase, however, the SOIR (PUT/CALL options ratio) which is around 0.8 points us to this sentiment around the company. This value is approximately 80% higher in 12 months.

A positive driver for buying Coinbase could be the news of the intention to withdraw the lawsuit against the company regarding the fact that the company should be regulated as an exchange. CEO Brian Armstrong posted on the X network that Coinbase has reached an agreement with the SEC staff to close their litigation case against the company. However, it still has to be voted on by the commission.

What does the latest results show us?

The recent data released absolutely blew away all analyst expectations. Despite the great results, Coinbase still has a lot of room to multiply its revenue, and in fact diversify, with the majority of its profits currently coming from stablecoin fees. Retail trading volume is below average and below 2021 levels. Coinbase could focus on asset tokenization, smart contracts, and cross-border payments. The relatively poor performance of the retail segment can be explained by the outflow of traders to meme coins, which the exchange has not kept up with issuing, but this was confirmed by Brian Armstrong himself on the X network. In the last few months, we can observe that the world has gone a bit crazy.

It's wild, and CoinMarketCap currently registers over 10 million digital assets. Brian Armstrong has stated that there are too many new coins being created, and judging the listing of each one is no longer feasible through the normal process. He referred to the company having to rely more often on reviews and use AI to evaluate projects. And this is where I see a lot of scope to further increase the company's profits. Despite poor data from the retail segment, the company was able to generate above-average revenue. Adding newly traded coins at a competitive pace could bring new users to Coinbase and lift the volume of traded funds significantly. The company certainly has room to grow in this regard, as it is ranked as high as #6 in the world based on the volume of funds traded in 24 hours.

Coinbase is one of the few exchanges that play different notes than its competitors. Coinbase charges significantly higher fees than its competitors, and this gives the company an edge in that sector. The premium that Coinbase is able to charge has increased significantly after the FTX crash. I expect that after the biggest heist in history on Bybit, Coinbase will be able to maintain and profit from this premium for a few more years. The company still maintains its reputation as a reliable place to buy and sell cryptocurrencies.

Other supporting news

At the end of January, the White House issued an order that included a mention that stablecoins backed by the US dollar should be supported. It is no secret that Trump is a supporter of cryptos and has plans to create a strategic reserve fund for Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD). Why is this significant? Because Coinbase's stock performance is still heavily influenced by the price of Bitcoin.

Recent market data has shown us that there is still no victory over inflation. Inflation appears to be sticky and Coinbase can benefit from that. Buying Coinbase can also be used as a form of hedge against the stagnation of rate cuts. Why? Because the company generates revenue by storing USDC reserves. As I mentioned above, Coinbase still needs to work on diversifying its revenue because more than 60% of the company's revenue is firmly tied to transaction fees. This means it will move much like the cryptocurrency market itself.

We are currently at prices that some larger portfolio managers were buying at. For example, Samantha McLemore added 15% to her current position, somewhere around the $240 per share price.

Jane Street Group has also been overbought near current levels. The last reported price at which they were adding to the portfolio was around $213 per share.

I would also like to mention the emerging lending market where crypto assets are being used as collateral. I believe there is still huge potential for growth in this segment. The pawn loan segment globally is approximately USD 4.3 trillion. With the increasing adoption of crypto assets, we will see more and more pledging of these assets. Coinbase has a big lead over its competitors as a provider of these loans. In early January, Coinbase partnered with Morpho Labs (the largest on-chain lending platform) to jointly launch a Bitcoin-backed lending service. The new service, which runs on Base, an Ethereum layer-2, allows users to borrow up to $100,000 in USDC using their Bitcoin as collateral, and is available to all US residents, paradoxically except for NY.

However, it's still cloudy

Coinbase in 2025 will still be an uncertain bet due to low revenue diversification. Steadying inflation and faster interest rate cuts by the central bank also remain a threat, which would result in a sharp decline in USDC revenue, which currently accounted for more than half of the increase in excellence. What we may perceive as a broad moat with Coinbase, namely that it operates in multiple areas such as asset management, broker, exchange, or lender, may, in fact, be the company's undoing. There may be a conflict of interest and damage to its reputation as a reliable haven for crypto asset trading.

We still don't know how the SEC will handle all the meme coins, if they designate them as unregistered securities then these activities would have to be suspended and my thesis of increased revenue from this area would burn down like a house of paper.

We have a fair value gap hanging on the chart that may be filled in the near term, and we may be looking at levels around $190 per share.

Conclusion

Despite the risks in the form of possible future regulations and the currently pessimistic sentiment about the company's future earnings generation, I see current levels as interesting to enter. I probably wouldn't give 100% of my intended portfolio allocation, but I will certainly invest a good first half, for example. The second half I will allocate if we look at levels between $170 and $190. Coinbase has a lot of room for improvement in the retail segment, where it can generate high revenues due to its reputation for reliability and charging a significant premium to plow its competitors. The recent heist on Bybit may be a catalyst for traders to think about where they want to hold and trade their funds, and the latter may perhaps lead to a new influx of users to Coinbase.

data.title: NOS, S.G.P.S., S.A. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by NOS, S.G.P.S., S.A. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Energy Recovery Growth Pipeline Advances Slowly, Warrants A Small Position
data.description:
Energy Recovery, Inc. (NASDAQ:ERII) has released Q4 2024 results, meeting full-year guidance and securing a critical quarter of revenue.

During the Investor's Day in November 2024, the company released moderate expectations for 2025/26, with acceleration in most verticals expected from 2027 onwards.

Given current valuation levels, I believe ERII now presents an attractive opportunity for a small initial position, based on adjusted earnings yield versus growth perspectives and execution risk. A small position allows flexibility to add if prices go lower, and provides some security against large execution risks embedded in growth expectations. I'm moving ERII to a Buy, with the risk warning that comes with growth expectations.

Q4 2024 Results And Forwards

Meeting guidance despite lumpiness: ERII successfully delivered on its desalination project pipeline. This was critical to meeting yearly guidance and even for generating any profits (all yearly profits were generated with Q4 2024 results). Given the lumpy nature of these projects, there was always a risk that delays could push revenues into 2025.

Investor Day projections: In November 2024, ERII hosted an investor day when it outlined guidance and market strategy through 2029. The roadmap projects moderate growth in the next two years before an expected acceleration in 2027 and beyond.

Desalination, reasonable projections with oil risk: ERII projects desalination growth of 8-10% CAGR through the decade, with slower revenue increases in 2025 and 2026 before accelerating in later years. This growth assumption is reasonable given the company’s strong competitive position (70% gross margins) and the increasing global need for desalination.

However, most projects still originate from the Middle East and North Africa. The primary risk here is macroeconomic, as desalination projects depend on funding, which often is tied to oil revenues from Gulf nations.

If oil prices drop sharply, project timelines could be affected.

Wastewater, riskier growth: ERII’s wastewater segment grew 85% YOY in 2024, reaching $12.6 million in revenue. This was impressive.

However, growth is expected to slow significantly to 20-25% in 2025-26, before reaccelerating to 30-40% later in the decade. This is indicative that the company's go-to-market is incomplete in this segment, and execution risk remains high.

Part of this stems from the company pursuing several industrial applications at the same time (municipal, mining, textile, industrial, and chemicals). Wastewater applications lack the same mission-critical status as desalination, and the smaller size of the operations may make the energy savings less impressive for the additional operational hassle.

In my opinion, despite having large revenues already, this segment carries a high risk in its growth prospects.

CO2 is the big bet: CO2 refrigeration remains the most significant long-term growth driver outside desalination.

The execution risk is still very high, but over the next 12–18 months, some of that risk should decline as two key OEM partners move into commercialization.

According to ERII's presentation, each is the smallest of the largest players in the U.S. and the EU, respectively. Makes sense that a smaller player tries to move faster into a new technology.

If ERII’s PX G1300 product gains traction with these OEMs, it could unlock meaningful revenue opportunities, from faster acceleration at larger OEMs. However, if major adoption hurdles emerge, the segment could struggle to scale. This segment is still in binary outcome mode.

Valuation Risks Are Compensated

Valuing ERII is complex because of its investment in new business lines.

The company projects $160 million in FY 2025 revenue, with 67% gross margins and $71 million in operating expenses. This translates to $35 million in pre-tax profit, or $31 million after tax (assuming a 10% tax rate due to R&D credits). Against an $830 million market cap, this is not particularly compelling.

However, ERII is reinvesting $25 million annually into new business lines (potentially up to $30 million+ when including the associated SG&A overhead).

If we add back this investment, ERII’s actual earnings capacity from water is closer to $50-55 million after taxes, implying a 6.6% yield on today’s valuation.

If ERII successfully grows at 10-15% CAGR, the earnings plus growth yield can reach the upper 15/20%.

Of course, this is highly risky because of execution. ERII has failed in opening new markets in the past (most importantly in oil in the late 2010s), and it has also succeeded (desalination).

I think the potential return in a positive scenario is high (15/20% and a company that generates 10% on earnings at the end of the decade while still growing at those figures), and in a negative scenario is somewhat compensated by the current earnings potential of the desalination business.

For that reason, I believe at these levels it is reasonable to initiate a small position, recognizing the high risk tied to the company’s expansion efforts. As either price declines or execution de-risks, a larger position could become more attractive over time.

data.title: Arbor Realty: This Chart Shows It's Time To Buy
data.description:
ABR stock: Previous thesis and chart of the day

I last covered Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) stock back in September 2023. As you can see from the following screenshot, that article was titled "Arbor Realty: The Dividend Lesson." In that article, I discussed ABR's appeal to income investors due to its 10%-plus yield and cautioned investors about the hidden risks. Quote:

Arbor Realty Trust, Inc. may appeal to income investors with its 10%-plus yield. Also, thanks to a few differentiating factors, Arbor Realty’s assets tend to be less volatile even during economic downturns compared to other leading mREIT stocks. However, you should be aware of the caveats hidden behind the 10%-plus yield. Its 10%-plus yield is not as attractive once properly benchmarked against historical averages and risk-free rates. The stock is currently trading at a considerable valuation premium and faces several profitability headwinds on the horizon.

The stock has indeed suffered a sizable price correction of -22% after that. When adjusted for dividend, the total return is still negative (-9.45%). In contrast, the S&P 500 enjoyed one of the strongest rallies during this period.

Besides the price changes, there have been a few fundamental catalysts evolving around ABR since my last writing too, which are the key factors that motivated this follow-up coverage. In the rest of this article, I will concentrate on the top two fundamental catalysts on my list: The changes in interest rates and also its latest dividend declaration. You will see why these catalysts have promoted me to upgrade the stock to a Buy rating from my earlier Hold rating.

In a nutshell, the combined effect of these changes is shown in the next chart – the chart of the day. The chart displays the spread between ABR dividend yield and the 10-Year Treasury Rate in the past 10 years. As seen, the current yield spread is 9.962%. It's not only far above the historical average spread of 7.051 but also is the third highest reading in at least 10 years, indicating extremely favorable risks premium when benchmarked against risk-free rates.

With this overview, let’s examine the stock in more details next.

ABR stock: Earnings outlook

Let me start with the negatives. ABR is certainly facing some headwinds (I will elaborate more later in the risk section), and I see good odds for its EPS to be volatile in the near term. As an example, ABR reported its latest quarterly earnings recently on Feb. 21, 2025. As you can see from the summary below, its earnings missed consensus across the board. Its normalized EPS was $0.40, missing estimates by $0.01. Revenue came in at $82.87 million, missing estimates by $1.61 million.

Looking forward, ABR's next quarterly earnings are scheduled in May and the estimates are quite pessimistic. As seen, the estimated normalized EPS is $0.32, translating into a sequential decline of 20%. All the EPS revisions are in the downward direction.

But I see some positives that can help offset the above negatives. First and foremost, I suggest investors focus more on the dividend payouts than accounting EPS. REIT stocks pay most of their economic earnings as dividends, and dividends are also free from the normal ambiguity associated with accounting earnings. In the case of ABR, it declared its latest quarterly dividend of $0.4300 per share for the March 2025 quarter as seen. This is in line with 2024, and payouts as seen, indicating stability of its economic earnings.

Another reason that made me feel optimistic of ABR earnings is that the yield curve inversion has ended recently. To wit, the chart below displays the long-short yield spread defined as the 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity minus the 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity in the past. At the time of my last writing in 2023, the yield curve was among the most inverted level in the past four decades. Recently, the spread of yield has turned positive and stayed so. As of this writing, the reading was a positive 0.20 as seen. A positive long-short spread tends to help mREIT stocks’ profits.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks, both in the upside and downside, are also worth mentioning. In terms of upside risks, the stock valuation has also become more attractive than the time of my last writing when measured by its Price to Book ratio (P/B ratio). As you can see from the following chart, its current P/B ratio is 0.972, compared a historical average of 1.104 and also a reading of 1.25 at the time of my last article.

In terms of downside risks, besides macroeconomic uncertainties, a particular risk evolving around ABR is its exposure to multifamily debt.

Overall, my conclusion is that positives are more than enough to offset the negatives under current conditions. To recap, the key positives that have developed since my last writing are the changes in interest rates and also ABR’s latest dividend declaration. These changes signal stability in ABR’s economic earnings and also pushed its yield spread relative to risk-free rates to the thickest levels in at least a decade. The changes in risk-free rates have also pulled the long-short yield spread to the positive regime. These changes thus have materially shifted the return/risk profile of ABR since my last writing, leading me to upgrade its rating to buy from my earlier hold rating.
